content.text: Git is the distributed version control system that underlies almost every modern software project, including the agent codebases, prompt libraries, and configuration repositories that power agentic AI systems. Learning Git's fundamentals — how it stores data, how you record changes, and how you synchronize with teammates — is the non-negotiable foundation for everything else in collaborative development.

version control system (DVCS) created by Linus Torvalds in 2005. Unlike centralized systems, every developer holds a complete copy of the repository — including its full history — on their local machine. There is no single server whose downtime blocks work. Collaboration happens by exchanging commits between copies.
At its core, Git manages a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of

. Each commit contains a snapshot of the entire repository at a point in time (stored efficiently as a tree of blob objects), a pointer to the previous commit(s), author metadata, and a message. This snapshot model — rather than storing diffs — is what makes Git's branching and merging so fast.
The three regions you interact with are: the

Agent systems are built from code, prompts, tool definitions, evaluation harnesses, and configuration files. All of these change over time as you tune behavior. Without version control you lose the ability to:
Roll back a prompt that degraded agent quality on a Monday morning deploy
Attribute a regression to the specific commit and author that introduced it
Run multiple prompt experiments in parallel without overwriting each other
Meet enterprise audit requirements: who changed what and when

Git also enables CI/CD pipelines: pushing a commit can trigger automated evals, linting, and deployment of the agent worker image — turning the repository into the single source of truth for what is running in production.
— every version of every file is recoverable
— creating a branch costs nanoseconds; no file copying
— commit, branch, and log work with no network
— every object is SHA-1/SHA-256 addressed; tampering is detectable
— GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, CI integrations, IDE plugins
— tag the exact SHA that was evaluated and deployed
— staging area and DAG model confuse new users
— model weights and large datasets need Git LFS or object storage
— rebase/force-push can confuse collaborators if misused
— concurrent edits to the same prompt file require manual resolution
— API keys committed accidentally remain in history forever without careful cleanup
When you edit a file, the change exists only in the
copies a snapshot of that file into the
packages everything in the index into a new commit object and writes it to the
object store. The staging area gives you precise control: you can add only part of a file's changes with

Each commit stores: the tree (snapshot), zero or more parent commit SHAs, author/committer name + email + timestamp, and the message. Git never mutates an existing object — it only creates new ones. A branch like
is simply a file containing the SHA of the latest commit on that branch.
is a file that contains the name of the current branch (or a commit SHA in detached-HEAD state).
A remote is a named reference to another repository URL.
is the conventional name for the first remote, usually GitHub.
downloads remote commits without touching your working directory.
The diagram below shows how data flows between the four zones in Git, and which commands move data in each direction.
Use Git for any project that will evolve over more than a single session. In practice this means:
— Python scripts, FastAPI services, agent orchestration code
— track system prompt versions alongside code; diff them like code
— YAML, TOML, JSON configs for tools and agent settings
— small golden-set JSONL files; large datasets use Git LFS or DVC
— secrets (.env), large model weights, compiled bytecode (__pycache__)

# Install Git (macOS via Homebrew, Linux via apt)
brew install git  # macOS
apt install git   # Ubuntu/Debian

# Configure your identity — this appears in every commit
git config --global user.name  "Deiva Mani"
git config --global user.email "deivamanibusiness@gmail.com"
git config --global init.defaultBranch main

# Use VS Code as the merge/diff editor
git config --global core.editor "code --wait"
git config --global merge.tool vscode

# Verify
git config --list

# Start a new local repository
mkdir my-agent && cd my-agent
git init          # creates .git/ directory

# -- OR -- clone an existing repo from GitHub
git clone https://github.com/yourorg/my-agent.git
cd my-agent

# Check the state of the working tree
git status

# See the commit log
git log --oneline --graph --decorate -10

# 1. Pull latest changes before starting work
git pull origin main

# 2. Edit files
echo 'You are a helpful coding assistant.' > prompts/system.txt

# 3. See what changed
git diff
git status

# 4. Stage specific files (or use -p for interactive hunks)
git add prompts/system.txt
git add src/tools/search.py

# 5. Review what will be committed
git diff --staged

# 6. Commit with a meaningful message
git commit -m "feat(prompt): clarify assistant persona in system prompt"

# 7. Push to remote
git push origin main

# Discard unstaged changes in a file
git restore prompts/system.txt

# Unstage a file (keep changes in working directory)
git restore --staged prompts/system.txt

# Amend the most recent commit message (before push only)
git commit --amend -m "fix(prompt): correct typo in assistant persona"

# Create a new commit that reverses a previous commit (safe for shared branches)
git revert HEAD

# Soft reset: keep changes staged (moves HEAD back one commit)
git reset --soft HEAD~1

# Hard reset: discard all changes since last commit (DESTRUCTIVE)
git reset --hard HEAD

# Concise graph view of the last 15 commits
git log --oneline --graph --decorate -15

# See what a specific commit changed
git show a3f9d12

# See who last changed each line of a file (blame)
git blame prompts/system.txt

# Compare working directory to the last commit
git diff HEAD

# Compare two commits
git diff HEAD~3 HEAD -- src/tools/search.py

# Search commit messages for a keyword
git log --all --oneline --grep="hallucination"

# Find when a bug was introduced with binary search
git bisect start
git bisect bad           # current commit is bad
git bisect good v1.3.0   # last known good tag
# Git checks out the midpoint; test and mark good/bad
git bisect good          # or: git bisect bad
git bisect reset         # when done
Setting up .gitignore for an agent project

# .gitignore — typical for a Python agent project
.env
.env.*
__pycache__/
*.pyc
*.pyo
.mypy_cache/
.ruff_cache/
venv/
.venv/
*.egg-info/
dist/
build/
# Large model files
*.bin
*.pt
*.safetensors
# Editor artifacts
.DS_Store
.vscode/settings.json
# Test outputs
.pytest_cache/
htmlcov/
# Generated data — track source JSONL, not derived parquet
data/processed/

# Apply to already-tracked files after editing .gitignore
git rm -r --cached .
git add .
git commit -m "chore: update .gitignore"
Python helper: automated git status checker

"""git_status.py — lightweight wrapper for programmatic git operations.

Useful in CI scripts and agent harnesses that need to inspect
repository state or ensure clean working trees before deployment.
"""
from __future__ import annotations
import subprocess
import sys
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from pathlib import Path


@dataclass
class GitStatus:
    branch: str
    ahead: int
    behind: int
    staged: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    unstaged: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    untracked: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    @property
    def is_clean(self) -> bool:
        return not (self.staged or self.unstaged or self.untracked)


def _run(cmd: list[str], cwd: Path | None = None) -> str:
    result = subprocess.run(
        cmd, cwd=cwd, capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
    )
    return result.stdout.strip()


def get_status(repo: Path = Path(".")) -> GitStatus:
    # Current branch
    branch = _run(["git", "rev-parse", "--abbrev-ref", "HEAD"], cwd=repo)

    # Ahead/behind remote tracking branch
    try:
        counts = _run(
            ["git", "rev-list", "--left-right", "--count", "@{u}...HEAD"],
            cwd=repo,
        )
        behind, ahead = (int(x) for x in counts.split())
    except subprocess.CalledProcessError:
        behind, ahead = 0, 0  # no upstream configured

    # Porcelain status
    raw = _run(["git", "status", "--porcelain"], cwd=repo)
    staged, unstaged, untracked = [], [], []
    for line in raw.splitlines():
        if not line:
            continue
        xy, path = line[:2], line[3:]
        x, y = xy[0], xy[1]
        if x == "?":      # untracked
            untracked.append(path)
        elif x != " ":   # staged change
            staged.append(path)
        if y not in (" ", "?"):  # unstaged change
            unstaged.append(path)

    return GitStatus(
        branch=branch,
        ahead=ahead,
        behind=behind,
        staged=staged,
        unstaged=unstaged,
        untracked=untracked,
    )


def assert_clean(repo: Path = Path(".")) -> None:
    """Raise SystemExit if the working tree is dirty."""
    status = get_status(repo)
    if not status.is_clean:
        print(f"ERROR: working tree is dirty on branch {status.branch!r}")
        if status.staged:
            print("  Staged:", status.staged)
        if status.unstaged:
            print("  Unstaged:", status.unstaged)
        if status.untracked:
            print("  Untracked:", status.untracked)
        sys.exit(1)
    if status.behind:
        print(f"WARNING: {status.behind} commit(s) behind upstream")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    s = get_status()
    print(f"Branch : {s.branch}  (+{s.ahead} / -{s.behind})")
    print(f"Clean  : {s.is_clean}")
    if not s.is_clean:
        print("Staged   :", s.staged)
        print("Unstaged :", s.unstaged)
        print("Untracked:", s.untracked)
A team is building a customer-support agent. They store the system prompt in
. When a product manager approves a new refund-handling flow, the engineer:
Pulls latest main to avoid stale-base conflicts
Edits prompts/system.txt and tools/refund.py with the new logic
Stages both files with git add; commits with a conventional message
Pushes the branch; CI runs the golden-eval suite; passes at 99.1%
Opens a PR; reviewer approves; squash-merges to main; tag v2.3.0
Deploy pipeline picks up the tag and pushes a new agent worker image
Two weeks later, refund escalations spike. The team runs
, finds the offending commit in 30 seconds, uses
to create a safe rollback commit, and is back in production within minutes.
— API keys in .env committed accidentally; use git-secrets or pre-commit hooks
— 'fix stuff' makes git log useless for debugging
— bypasses review and CI gates; use branches
— rewrites history colleagues are already building on
— one commit per logical change; easier to bisect and revert
— accepting either side blindly without re-running tests
Use Conventional Commits: feat:, fix:, chore:, docs:, test: — enables changelogs and semantic versioning
Write .gitignore before making your first commit; update it when adding tools
Commit often while developing; squash before merging to keep main history clean
Always git pull before starting new work to avoid divergent histories
Use git diff --staged before every commit to verify exactly what you are recording
Tag releases that go to production: git tag -a v1.0.0 -m 'Production release'
Never git reset --hard on shared branches; use git revert instead
Use pre-commit hooks (pre-commit library) to run lint and secret scanning before every commit
Git is fast for source code because it compresses objects using pack files and delta encoding. However, it does not handle large binary files well:
— stores large files (model weights, audio) on a separate object server; only pointers go in .git/
— git clone --depth 1 for CI builds that only need the latest snapshot
— check out only a subtree of a monorepo
— runs garbage collection and repacking; hosts run this automatically
— layer on top of Git for ML datasets and model artifacts, similar to LFS but pipeline-aware
Most popular; free for public repos
gh pr create, gh issue list, etc.
Lint, secret scan, format on save
For model weights, audio, video files
IDE integrations: VS Code's Source Control panel, JetBrains' Git tab, and vim-fugitive all wrap the same
CLI. The CLI is always the ground truth.
Smaller ecosystem; rare in new projects
— install git-secrets or gitleaks as a pre-commit hook; secrets in history require full history rewrite (BFG Repo Cleaner)
— GPG/SSH signing proves authorship; require it on main via branch protection rules
— require PR approval + passing CI before merge; prevent force-push to main
— pin dependencies by hash not version tag; automated PRs from Dependabot/Renovate keep them updated
— Git's content-addressed storage means any tampering changes all downstream SHAs — corruption is detectable
— push to GitHub as primary and a self-hosted mirror for disaster recovery

. Each commit is a photograph of your entire project at one moment. Branches let you create alternate timelines. The staging area is like a camera viewfinder — you choose exactly which changes appear in the next photograph before pressing the shutter (
). Pushing shares your timeline with teammates; pulling merges their timeline into yours.
The staging area is not a mistake — it is Git's superpower. It lets you craft clean, atomic commits even from a messy working directory.
Git stores snapshots, not diffs — branches and merges are cheap because of this
The core cycle is: edit → git add → git commit → git push
The staging area gives you control over exactly what goes into each commit
A branch is just a movable pointer to a commit — HEAD tracks the active branch
Never commit secrets; use .gitignore and pre-commit hooks from day one
Use git revert to safely undo changes on shared branches; reserve reset for private history
Agent teams should track prompts, tools, and configs in Git alongside code — treat text artifacts as code
